decks.title: Lecture 1 - Geometry, Equilibria, Boozer Coordinates, Metrics

slides.title: What can we now optimize directly?
bullets: Equilibria, spectra, transport metrics, coils, profiles, and design decisions
The lecture sequence follows the same computational object through each step
slides.subtitle: Lecture 1: geometry, equilibria, Boozer coordinates, metrics

slides.title: PART 1. From magnetic confinement to optimization variables
bullets: Use geometry to define the search space
Use computations to decide what changed

slides.title: Stellarator optimization starts with a parameterization
bullets: Boundary Fourier modes
Pressure and current profiles
Aspect ratio, iota, volume, beta, magnetic well

slides.title: The inverse problem starts with choices
bullets: Choose variables before choosing an optimizer
State which physics model is active
Record assumptions before comparing designs

slides.title: What the optimizer sees
bullets: Inputs: boundary and radial profiles
Outputs: nested surfaces, rotational transform, magnetic diagnostics
Artifacts: wout file, figures, status line

slides.title: Boundary Fourier modes control the shape
bullets: Small changes move symmetry, aspect ratio, and resonances together
Use mode scans to teach coupling before live optimization

slides.title: Profiles are part of the equilibrium object
bullets: Pressure and current profiles change rotational transform and stability
A boundary-only story is a first approximation

slides.title: VMEC is the first computational gate
bullets: Input: boundary and radial profiles
Output: nested-surface equilibrium and wout file
Teaching fallback: cached surface and iota profile

slides.title: The wout file is a checked artifact
bullets: Inspect dimensions, variables, iota profile, aspect ratio, and surface geometry
A plot without the file provenance is incomplete

slides.title: Example: HSX-like QHS surface
bullets: Small stellarator case for classroom calculations
Natural quasisymmetry example
Use cached figures for live robustness

slides.title: Example: rotational transform profile
bullets: Iota controls field-line pitch and resonance exposure
Use changes in iota as a first optimization diagnostic

slides.title: Equilibrium convergence is a result
bullets: Non-convergence contains information
Bad resolution can create attractive false metrics
Cached mode keeps the lecture moving while preserving the warning

slides.title: Notebook project: first equilibrium object
bullets: Load the HSX wout file when available
Plot LCFS and iota profile
Change one boundary-mode proxy and inspect metric coupling

slides.title: PART 2. Boozer coordinates turn geometry into spectra
bullets: Represent magnetic-field strength in useful coordinates
Separate symmetry-preserving modes from symmetry-breaking modes

slides.title: Boozer coordinates make symmetry measurable
bullets: Many design targets are statements about |B| in special coordinates
A spectrum converts geometry into a table of modes

slides.title: Example: B(theta,zeta) contour
bullets: Clean bands suggest dominant intended symmetry
Inspect the contour before reading one scalar residual

slides.title: Example: Boozer spectrum heatmap
bullets: Dominant symmetry-line modes carry the design target
Off-line modes define penalties and diagnostics

slides.title: Several symmetry targets use the same workflow
bullets: Quasi-axisymmetry, quasi-helical symmetry, and quasi-isodynamicity differ by mode structure
The residual must match the intended target

slides.title: Boozer transforms need a convergence audit
bullets: Which modes were retained?
Which surfaces were used?
Was transform convergence checked?

slides.title: Notebook project: Boozer spectrum
bullets: Generate B(theta,zeta) from cached modes
Identify symmetry-breaking modes
Perturb one bad mode and watch the residual rise

slides.title: PART 3. Metrics turn spectra into design objectives
bullets: Compress the spectrum into a useful scalar
Keep the full plot next to the scalar

slides.title: Effective ripple is an optimization metric
bullets: Compresses low-collisionality neoclassical transport risk
Useful as a screen before expensive transport calculations

slides.title: Example: effective-ripple comparison
bullets: Profile shapes matter: edge and core can rank differently
One scalar hides where the risk lives

slides.title: Example: sensitivity to one geometry knob
bullets: A useful metric tells the optimizer which direction is worse
Finite differences are enough for classroom intuition

slides.title: Gradients expand the design space
bullets: Finite differences are robust for teaching
Adjoints and autodiff become important for large searches
Gradient quality must be checked against physics fidelity

slides.title: Autodiff changes the coding contract
bullets: Every operation in the objective must be differentiable or isolated
Cached mode teaches structure before research-mode autodiff tests

slides.title: The scalar objective is a negotiation
bullets: Magnetic quality, profiles, coil risk, and transport gates compete
Weights encode values, program stage, and risk choices

slides.title: Metric definitions need units and provenance
bullets: Definition of the residual
Validation domain
Sensitivity direction
Known failure mode

slides.title: Data provenance is a scientific result
bullets: Real public HSX and W7-X files are fetched by script
Synthetic/cached arrays are labeled and documented in STATUS.md

slides.title: Computational checkpoint for lecture 1
bullets: Run the environment check
Fetch equilibria
Generate figures
Open notebooks 01-03

slides.title: Exercise: change a boundary mode
bullets: Predict which metrics move first
Regenerate the surface and mode-scan figure
State what the toy model can prove

slides.title: Exercise: circle the bad Boozer modes
bullets: Which modes preserve the target symmetry?
Which modes would you penalize?
What happens if one bad mode triples?

slides.title: Checklist: VMEC file review
bullets: dimensions
iota
boundary coefficients
volume/aspect
beta and pressure-current profiles

slides.title: Checklist: Boozer file review
bullets: surface labels
nfp
mode truncation
dominant and off-symmetry modes

bullets: Use artifacts with provenance

decks.title: Lecture 2 - Coils, Quadratic Flux, Single-Stage Optimization

slides.title: From plasma boundary to magnetic coils
bullets: Stage-2 coil design translates a target field into hardware
The central diagnostic is normal-field error together with coil complexity
slides.subtitle: Lecture 2: coils, quadratic flux, single-stage optimization

slides.title: PART 1. Stage 1, stage 2, and single-stage design
bullets: Stage 1 asks which plasma boundary is attractive
Stage 2 asks which coils can produce it
Single-stage design changes plasma and coils together

slides.title: What is single-stage optimization?
bullets: Stage 1: plasma shape
Stage 2: coil shapes
Single stage: expanded degrees of freedom

slides.title: The coil inverse problem is ill-posed
bullets: Many current distributions can approximate the same field
Regularization selects the buildable solution

slides.title: Quadratic flux is the basic stage-2 score
bullets: Measure normal-field error on the target surface
Low B dot n means the field respects the requested boundary

slides.title: Current-potential methods regularize a surface current
bullets: NESCOIL and REGCOIL solve for currents on a winding surface
Regularization trades field accuracy for smoother current patterns

slides.title: Filamentary coils convert fields into curves
bullets: Curves have length, curvature, torsion, and separation
A field target can be easy for a surface current and difficult for discrete coils

slides.title: SIMSOPT is the mature stage-2 teaching path
bullets: Geometry objects, curves, Biot-Savart fields, objectives, optimization loops
Research mode should use verified public QA input

slides.title: Notebook project: SIMSOPT stage-2 coils
bullets: Load public QA input or cached coil fallback
Plot initial and final coils
Compare normal-field error before and after

slides.title: Example: initial coil set
bullets: The teaching curves are simple enough to inspect
Their main lesson is the diagnostic grammar

slides.title: Example: final coil set
bullets: A coil plot without diagnostics is incomplete
Students should ask: length, curvature, clearance, ports

slides.title: Example: normal-field error before and after
bullets: Before/after maps show where the target surface is damaged
Local hot spots matter for robustness

slides.title: Example: regularization tradeoff
bullets: Shorter and smoother coils can worsen field accuracy
A single optimum hides a design choice

slides.title: Coil complexity has multiple dimensions
bullets: Length
Maximum curvature
Mean-squared curvature
Torsion
Coil-to-coil and coil-to-surface separation

slides.title: Ports and maintenance enter early
bullets: Access constraints can invalidate attractive coil sets
Use engineering constraints as penalties or hard gates

slides.title: HTS tapes add orientation and strain constraints
bullets: A reactor coil is more than a mathematical filament
Curvature and force metrics eventually become mechanical checks

slides.title: PART 2. Fieldline and particle diagnostics
bullets: Coils can match a surface and still change topology
Fieldline and particle diagnostics catch late-stage surprises

slides.title: Fieldlines diagnose topology
bullets: Reveal islands, stochasticity, and topology changes
Fieldline plots are followed by particle diagnostics

slides.title: Notebook project: ESSOS fieldlines and particles
bullets: Plot cached fieldline and orbit diagnostics
Use ESSOS in research mode only after timing
Discuss fast-particle relevance for reactors

slides.title: Example: cached field-line diagnostic
bullets: Good fieldlines support the coil story
Bad topology catches failures a scalar map can miss

slides.title: Example: particle orbit cartoon
bullets: Fast-ion and alpha-particle losses are reactor gates
Guiding-center and full-orbit fidelity should be chosen deliberately

slides.title: Two-stage optimization can change the plasma target
bullets: Stage 1 may find a boundary that no reasonable coil set can make
Stage 2 may return coils that damage the original objective

slides.title: PART 3. Single-stage optimization
bullets: Move plasma and coil variables together
Use continuation to keep the problem teachable

slides.title: Single-stage design keeps coils in the loop
bullets: Boundary variables and coil variables move together
The objective includes plasma metrics, flux error, and coil metrics

slides.title: Notebook project: single-stage toy
bullets: Plot objective pieces separately
Vary the flux penalty weight
Identify the penalty that prevents unbuildable solutions

slides.title: Example: single-stage objective history
bullets: The total objective can decrease while one term stalls
Plot terms together with the sum

slides.title: Example: weight continuation
bullets: Start from an easier problem
Increase coupling or constraint weight
Watch which term starts controlling the solution

slides.title: Direct coil optimization changes the gradient story
bullets: Biot-Savart fields are differentiable
Fieldline and particle objectives can be differentiated in JAX workflows
Gradient accuracy still needs validation

slides.title: Autodiff still needs validation
bullets: Autodiff gives derivatives of the implemented model
The implemented model still has a validity domain

slides.title: Robustness is a stage-2 metric
bullets: Manufacturing errors
Current perturbations
Coil misalignment
Maintenance tolerances

slides.title: A coil objective needs units and scales
bullets: Length in meters
Curvature in inverse meters
Flux error normalized to field scale
Separation in centimeters

slides.title: Computational checkpoint for lecture 2
bullets: Run notebooks 04, 05, 06 in cached mode
Regenerate coil and fieldline figures
Record which paths are cached and which are live

slides.title: Exercise: increase the length penalty
bullets: What improves?
What worsens?
Which metric would you plot before choosing?

slides.title: Exercise: choose a coil set
bullets: Design A: lower B dot n, higher curvature
Design B: smoother, worse flux
Which one survives a first experiment?

bullets: 04 stage-2 coils
05 single-stage toy
06 fieldlines and particles
11 Pareto preview

slides.title: Five rules from lecture 2
bullets: Pair every boundary with coils
Plot B dot n maps
Keep coil complexity explicit
Use continuation for coupled objectives
Treat engineering as a gate

slides.title: Transport physics becomes optimization metrics
bullets: Neoclassical, turbulence, and fast particles enter as screens and validation gates
The goal is to use the physics responsibly inside a design loop
slides.subtitle: Lecture 3: neoclassical, turbulence, fast-particle metrics

slides.title: PART 1. A hierarchy of transport calculations
bullets: Cheap screens remove poor designs early
Expensive calculations validate finalists

slides.title: Cost hierarchy for transport metrics
bullets: Geometry metrics are fast
Boozer and effective-ripple metrics screen designs
DKE, gyrokinetics, particles, and profiles validate finalists

slides.title: Neoclassical recap for optimizers
bullets: Symmetry breaking produces trapped-particle radial drifts
Effective ripple compresses one transport risk
Ambipolar electric fields and bootstrap current matter

slides.title: Example: effective ripple comparison
bullets: Useful: fast, profile-like, interpretable
Limit: still followed by transport matrices or experiments

slides.title: NEO-JAX and SFINCS-JAX have different roles
bullets: NEO_JAX: compact effective-ripple diagnostics
SFINCS_JAX: richer drift-kinetic validation

slides.title: Notebook project: SFINCS-JAX cached validation
bullets: Read a collisionality scan
Inspect Er roots
Plot a bootstrap-current proxy

slides.title: Example: cached neoclassical scan
bullets: Transport coefficients change across regimes
Suppression mechanisms are profile and field dependent

slides.title: Example: ambipolar Er roots
bullets: Multiple roots can imply different impurity and turbulence behavior
Optimization records the root choice

slides.title: Example: bootstrap-current constraint
bullets: Bootstrap current can change equilibrium and island risk
It depends on profiles and collisionality

slides.title: W7-X motivates neoclassical optimization
bullets: Reduced neoclassical losses are a stellarator-design success
The next bottleneck can still be turbulence

slides.title: PART 2. Turbulence metrics
bullets: Use linear calculations as early screens
Compare with nonlinear validation when rankings matter

slides.title: The turbulence question is a fidelity ladder
bullets: Linear growth: fast screen
Quasilinear weights: ranking hypothesis
Nonlinear heat flux: expensive validation
Profiles: performance closure

slides.title: Notebook project: SPECTRAX-GK linear metric
bullets: Plot growth rate versus ky rho_i
Plot mode frequency
State the range of validity of the proxy

slides.title: Example: linear growth-rate spectrum
bullets: Growth-rate peaks indicate instability drive
They are followed by nonlinear heat-flux checks

slides.title: Example: linear mode frequency
bullets: Growth and frequency together are more informative than growth alone
The sign and trend help identify mode families

slides.title: Notebook project: proxy versus validation
bullets: Choose the best design by each metric
Compare the rankings
Discuss why rankings differ

slides.title: Example: proxy versus nonlinear validation
bullets: A proxy may rank designs differently from nonlinear validation
The optimizer receives whatever metric you give it

slides.title: Example: W7-X ion-temperature-gradient clamping
bullets: Neoclassical success can leave turbulent limits
Profile response can saturate performance

slides.title: PART 3. Fast particles and validation gates
bullets: Reactors require alpha-particle confinement
Particle metrics must be rerun after coil design changes

slides.title: Fast particles are reactor gates
bullets: Present experiments may tolerate losses that reactors cannot
Alpha confinement, wall loads, and orbit classes enter late-stage validation

slides.title: Particle metrics connect back to coils
bullets: Coil errors can alter field topology
Fast-particle diagnostics should be rerun after coil design changes

slides.title: Metric trust ladder
bullets: screen
ranking hypothesis
validation calculation
experiment or integrated scenario

slides.title: Validation compares failure modes
bullets: Does the metric fail on a known bad design?
Does it rank a known good design correctly?
Does it change smoothly under small perturbations?

slides.title: Computational checkpoint for lecture 3
bullets: Run notebooks 07, 08, 09
Record which curves are cached
Replace one cached column only if a real run is timed

slides.title: Exercise: choose early and late metrics
bullets: Which metric screens 1000 designs?
Which metric validates five finalists?
What is the failure mode of each?

slides.title: Exercise: proxy versus nonlinear ranking
bullets: Select the proxy optimum
Select the validation optimum
Explain why the optimizer might prefer the wrong one

slides.title: Five rules from lecture 3
bullets: Use cheap screens early
Validate with richer physics late
Plot profiles and regimes
Never hide proxy failure
Record the trust ladder

decks.title: Lecture 4 - Profiles, Pareto Fronts, Repo Lab

slides.title: From optimized field to credible design workflow
bullets: Profiles, coils, transport, and decisions must be checked together
The final deliverable is an auditable design argument
slides.subtitle: Lecture 4: profiles, Pareto fronts, repo lab

slides.title: PART 1. Close the loop with profiles
bullets: Field optimization changes transport
Transport changes profiles
Profiles change objectives

slides.title: The full loop has memory
bullets: Geometry changes transport
Transport changes profiles
Profiles change objectives
Coils and engineering constrain what survives

slides.title: Profiles close the design loop
bullets: Temperature and density gradients drive transport
Sources and sinks close the performance claim

slides.title: NEOPAX or fallback profile closure
bullets: Research path: profile-oriented JAX workflows
Cached path: educational radial diffusion/source model

slides.title: Notebook project: profile closure
bullets: Plot Ti, Te, and source balance
Increase turbulent transport
Explain why field-only optimization is incomplete

slides.title: Example: profile closure fallback
bullets: Extra turbulent transport flattens the ion-temperature profile
A good field metric can still underperform after closure

slides.title: Example: power-balance check
bullets: Sources, fluxes, and profiles must be mutually consistent
A profile plot without a balance check is weak evidence

slides.title: Integrated objectives should be staged
bullets: Early: cheap geometry and Boozer screens
Middle: coils and neoclassical metrics
Late: turbulence, particles, profiles, engineering

slides.title: Reactor constraints enter as gates
bullets: Alpha heating and losses
Wall loads and blankets
Tritium breeding
Maintenance and access
Materials and forces

slides.title: Coil robustness returns in the final workflow
bullets: Manufacturing tolerances
Current errors
Thermal and mechanical deformation
Field-error correction margin

slides.title: PART 2. Pareto decisions
bullets: Many designs can be defensible
The decision weights must be explicit

slides.title: Several good designs can coexist
bullets: Better transport can mean harder coils
Easier coils can mean worse turbulence or profiles
Decision weights must be explicit

slides.title: Notebook project: Pareto dashboard
bullets: Load cached design table
Compute nondominated points
Change weights and watch the selected design move

slides.title: Example: Pareto front
bullets: Nondominated points still require priorities
They are designs worth arguing about

slides.title: Example: weighted selection dashboard
bullets: Weights encode priorities, risk tolerance, and program stage
Changing them should be visible and reproducible

slides.title: Validation gates prevent metric gaming
bullets: equilibrium
Boozer
neoclassical
coils
turbulence
particles
profiles
engineering

slides.title: PART 3. The GitHub repo as the lab manual
bullets: Slides explain concepts
Notebooks execute projects
Scripts regenerate figures, movies, and status

slides.title: The GitHub repo is the lab manual
bullets: Slides explain concepts
Notebooks execute projects
Scripts regenerate figures, movies, and status
STATUS.md records truthfulness

slides.title: Cached, tiny, and research modes serve different audiences
bullets: cached: student reliability
tiny: instructor live demo
research: real-code replacement after timing

slides.title: CI is part of teaching reproducibility
bullets: Cached-mode CI runs smoke tests, data fetch, figures, movies, notebooks, and pytest
Students can trust a green baseline before experimenting

slides.title: Notebook project: full repo run
bullets: Run check_release_ready.py
Run execute_all_notebooks.py
Review STATUS.md before making claims

slides.title: Design sprint: modify one objective
bullets: Choose one metric column
Change a weight or cached value
Regenerate the plot
Explain whether the design decision changed

slides.title: Design sprint: replace one cached artifact
bullets: Pick a real package output
Write it to data/cached with provenance
Update STATUS.md and rerun tests

slides.title: How students should use the repo after the school
bullets: Start cached
Change one notebook at a time
Keep generated outputs small
Document live-code failures honestly

slides.title: How researchers should use the repo
bullets: Replace fallback arrays with real outputs
Preserve notebook structure
Add tests for new artifacts
Avoid overclaiming new scientific results

slides.title: What belongs in git
bullets: small public inputs
small cached data
figures used by slides
notebooks and scripts
status and docs

slides.title: What stays out of git
bullets: prompt-package PDFs/PPTX/zips
private screenshots
large generated runs
tokens or local environments

slides.title: Integrated workflows need ownership
bullets: Every artifact needs an owner: script, notebook, source, and status line
This makes the workflow teachable and debuggable

slides.title: Pareto fronts need interpretation
bullets: A Pareto front is an argument surface
The final choice still needs context and validation

slides.title: Final design commandments
bullets: Show every boundary with its coil story
State the validation domain for every metric
Pair each proxy with its failure mode
Show the weights behind each Pareto choice

slides.title: Keep cached mode honest
bullets: Label synthetic data
Use only real wout files or labeled synthetic fallbacks
Record limitations in STATUS.md

slides.title: Make every plot teach a decision
bullets: What changed?
Which design wins?
Which failure mode appears?
What would a higher-fidelity code check?

slides.title: Future of stellarator optimization
bullets: Coupled
Differentiable
Multi-fidelity
Profile-aware
Reproducible
